title: Strong El Niño event will have wide-ranging impacts on energy
gist: A weekly Wood Mackenzie energy roundup led by a forecast 'very strong' El Niño (81% NOAA probability) that could hit US/Iberian solar output, cut Panama Canal draft limits, and mildify US/European winter gas demand, alongside a flare-up in US-Iran tensions that pushed European TTF gas up 15% and Brent up 4% since 1 July, plus notes on SPR draws, RGGI carbon prices surging 76% YTD, and Virginia data-centre power demand.

themes.theme: Strong El Niño forecast and energy sector impacts

insights.claim: NOAA said there was an 81% chance of a 'very strong' El Niño in the last quarter of 2026, which if realized would rank among the largest El Niño events since the historical record began in 1950.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A top-tier El Niño event carries outsized, quantifiable risk to renewable generation, gas demand and shipping logistics that energy investors should price into Q4 2026 forecasts.
insights.quote: The US National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Administration said this week there was an 81% chance of a "very strong" El Niño in the last quarter of this year.

insights.claim: The 2023-2024 El Niño reduced wind output in the central US and solar output in California, caused steep hydro generation declines in China and India, and made that winter the warmest on record for the Lower 48 US states.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Provides a concrete historical precedent for how the current strong El Niño could suppress renewable generation output across multiple major markets simultaneously.

insights.claim: Scientists suggest the stronger the El Niño, the greater the impact on solar generation, meaning the hit to solar output in the southern and western US could be the largest yet seen if the forecast strong event materializes.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Solar-heavy US regions (California, Southwest) face potential record generation shortfalls in 2026-2027, a risk factor for utility-scale solar asset output forecasts and power price volatility.

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's Eugene Kim notes a strong El Niño could mean a mild winter for 2026-2027 in the northern US, and possibly Europe too, which could be important for gas demand.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A mild 2026-2027 winter would suppress heating-driven gas demand in both the US and Europe, a bearish factor for winter gas price spikes that traders should weigh against the bullish Middle East supply risk.

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's maritime research director Matthew Wheatley says preparation for El Niño-driven shipping disruption is not optional but the cost of maintaining operational continuity.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Signals shipping/maritime operators should proactively budget for El Niño-related routing and canal-draft disruptions rather than treating them as a tail risk.
insights.quote: In an El Niño year, preparation is not optional. It is the cost of maintaining operational continuity.

themes.theme: Panama Canal draft restrictions and LNG shipping

insights.claim: The 2023-2024 El Niño-driven drought forced the Panama Canal Authority to reduce the Neopanamax locks' maximum allowable draft from a typical ~15 metres to below 14 metres for most of H2 2023-H1 2024, at times as low as 13.26 metres.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Quantifies the historical scale of Panama Canal draft restrictions, giving investors a baseline for estimating potential shipping cost/delay impacts if the current strong El Niño repeats similar drought conditions.

insights.claim: 2023-2024 Panama Canal draft restrictions forced many LNG carriers heading for Asia to reroute via the Cape of Good Hope or Suez Canal, adding two to three weeks to journey times.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A repeat of these restrictions under the new strong El Niño would raise LNG shipping costs and transit times to Asia, a bullish factor for Asian LNG landed prices and tanker day rates.

insights.claim: The Panama Canal Authority is again cutting the maximum allowed draft due to expected weather conditions including the approaching El Niño, though the new limit (currently ~14.9 metres, falling to 14.5 metres on 15 August) remains higher than the 2023-2024 restrictions.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Confirms draft restrictions are already being reinstated ahead of the forecast El Niño peak; investors should monitor whether limits fall further toward 2023-2024 levels as a leading indicator of shipping disruption severity.

themes.theme: Gulf conflict flares but stops short of full war

insights.claim: Iran hit three tankers with projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz, prompting the US to strike about 90 targets in Iran and cancel a temporary sanctions waiver that had allowed Iran to sell oil for 60 days; Iran responded by targeting US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Qatar.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: An active tit-for-tat military exchange around Hormuz, even short of full war, sustains geopolitical risk premium in oil and gas prices and keeps tanker rerouting/insurance costs elevated.

insights.claim: President Trump declared the mid-June Memorandum of Understanding with Iran 'over,' calling further dealings with Iran 'a waste of time,' though US officials reportedly continued working toward a long-term peace.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Public rhetoric suggesting the US-Iran MoU has collapsed, even as back-channel talks continue, signals continued volatility risk for oil/gas prices tied to Gulf de-escalation headlines.
insights.quote: As far as I'm concerned, it's just a waste of time dealing with them. They're liars.

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's Vesseltracker service identified about 50 vessels per day passing through the Strait of Hormuz this week, down from about 170 a day before the war but higher than the 10-20 a day seen during the height of the conflict before the MoU.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, trackable real-time metric (vessel transit counts) for gauging the severity of Hormuz disruption at any point in time, useful for shipping and oil market monitoring.

insights.claim: Tanker movements through the Strait of Hormuz might not return to pre-war levels even if Gulf production is fully restored, because Saudi Arabia and the UAE have permanently increased oil exports via alternative routes, and Saudi Arabia is reportedly considering expanding capacity on its East-West pipeline to Yanbu on the Red Sea.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A structural, permanent diversion of Gulf oil exports away from Hormuz would durably reduce the strait's chokepoint risk premium over time, a long-term bearish factor for Hormuz-risk pricing even after the current conflict resolves.

insights.claim: The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve fell to its lowest level since 1983 after dropping 6.2 million barrels in the prior week, underlining the risk to the global economy if Gulf export disruption persists.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: SPR levels at a four-decade low reduce the US government's buffer capacity to counter further oil price spikes from Gulf disruption, raising the stakes of any escalation.

insights.claim: European benchmark TTF gas for August delivery traded at about US$16.50/mmbtu on Friday morning, up about 15% since 1 July, while front-month Brent crude was about US$76/barrel, up 4% over the same period, with the gas price move sharper than the oil move.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: European gas is repricing Gulf/Hormuz risk more aggressively than oil, suggesting European gas security remains the more acute near-term vulnerability from the conflict.
insights.locator: prices for oil and gas rebounded paragraph

themes.theme: US grid strain and carbon market moves

insights.claim: The US Department of Energy's latest Transmission Needs Study warns of increasing operational challenges for the grid, citing load growth from new data centres, cybersecurity and physical risks, and aging transmission infrastructure combined with a changing generation mix.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: An official DOE report flagging data-centre-driven grid strain corroborates the broader thesis that AI power demand is outpacing US transmission capacity, a systemic risk for grid reliability investors should track.
insights.locator: In brief section, Transmission Needs Study paragraph

insights.claim: RGGI (US Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative) carbon allowance prices have risen about 76% year-to-date, rising above California's carbon price for the first time, partly due to anticipation of Virginia's re-entry into the RGGI market on 1 July 2026.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A 76% YTD surge in RGGI prices, driven by Virginia's return, is a major carbon-market repricing event relevant to power generators and carbon allowance traders in the US Northeast.

insights.claim: The AI boom is driving strong power demand growth in Virginia, the world's largest data centre hub, with a significant proportion of that additional demand expected to be met by fossil fuel generation, creating additional demand for emissions allowances.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Directly links AI data centre growth in Virginia to fossil-fuel generation and rising carbon allowance demand, a mechanism investors can use to connect AI capex growth to RGGI price upside.
